source_case: Cepeda v. Cumberland Engineering Co., 76 N.J. 152 (1978)
doctrinal_framework: Wade seven-factor risk-utility test (New Jersey adoption)
case_background: Plaintiff Cepeda, a factory worker, operated a pelletizing machine manufactured by Cumberland Engineering. The machine had a point-of-operation hazard — the intake port where plastic feedstock is pushed into rollers for pellet formation — and Cepeda's fingers were drawn into the rollers and amputated. He sued Cumberland on design-defect grounds, arguing that an interlock guard preventing hand insertion into the intake was a feasible safer design. The New Jersey Supreme Court adopted the seven Wade risk-utility factors and remanded for jury consideration of design defect under the new test.

factors.text: The usefulness and desirability of the pelletizing machine to industrial users — essential equipment for plastic processing, with substantial commercial utility.
factors.weight_justification: Court recognized substantial industrial utility but noted that utility alone does not defeat defect claims

factors.text: The likelihood of operator injury during foreseeable use of the pelletizing machine, given the physical design of the intake port and the nature of factory work rhythms.
factors.weight_justification: Court found the probability non-trivial given repetitive-hand-motion factory tasks

factors.text: The probable seriousness of injury from the pelletizing machine's intake rollers — finger amputation, hand injury, permanent disability.
factors.weight_justification: Severe — amputation is among the most serious foreseeable industrial injuries

factors.text: The availability of a safer alternative design — specifically an interlock guard that physically prevents hand insertion into the intake, or a presence-detection sensor that stops the rollers when a hand approaches.
factors.weight_justification: Interlock guards were commercially available and well-known in 1978 industrial equipment design

factors.text: The cost and feasibility of incorporating an interlock guard into the pelletizing machine design — manufacturing cost, impact on throughput, maintenance burden.
factors.weight_justification: Cost was modest but throughput impact was contested

factors.text: Factory worker's anticipated awareness of the point-of-operation hazard — whether the risk is obvious to an ordinary operator or concealed by the machine's design.
factors.weight_justification: Industrial operators have some expected awareness of machinery hazards, but not dispositive

factors.text: Feasibility of spreading the loss via product price increases and manufacturer liability insurance, as compared with placing the loss on the injured worker.
factors.weight_justification: Manufacturers can spread industrial-equipment-injury costs through pricing and insurance

question: Under the seven-factor Wade risk-utility test, was the Cumberland Engineering pelletizing machine defectively designed because it lacked an interlock guard at the intake port?

ground_truth.rationale: The New Jersey Supreme Court reversed the trial court's directed verdict for the defendant and remanded under the newly articulated Wade seven-factor test, holding that the plaintiff could reach the jury on design defect. The availability of a safer alternative design (interlock guard) combined with the severity of foreseeable injury and the non-trivial probability of occurrence outweighed the moderate burden and user-awareness factors.
